1: Josiah was eight years old at accession (c. 640 BCE) and reigned thirty-one years until 609 BCE when he died at Megiddo. His reign is the last great reform before the Babylonian exile. The Chronicler’s treatment of Josiah is parallel to his treatment of Hezekiah — both are faithful kings who inherit corrupted kingdoms and implement comprehensive reforms. The length of the reign (31 years) is notable given his young accession age.
2: “Walking in the ways of his ancestor David” (wēyyēlek bĕdarkê dāwîd ʾāḇîw) is the Chronicler’s highest royal evaluation, reserved for Asa (2 Chronicles 14:2), Hezekiah (2 Chronicles 29:2), and Josiah. “Not turning aside to the right or to the left” is the Deuteronomic formula for complete obedience to Torah (Deuteronomy 5:32; 2 Chronicles 17:11; Joshua 1:7). The phrase frames Josiah as the ideal Deuteronomic king of Deuteronomy 17:18-20, who reads the law and follows it precisely.
3: The Chronicler provides a staged timeline for Josiah’s reform absent from 2 Kings: (1) eighth year of reign (age 16) — “he began to seek the God of his ancestor David” (ḥēl lidroš lēʾlōhê dāwîd ʾāḇîw); (2) twelfth year (age 20) — “he began to purge Judah and Jerusalem.” The verb dāraš (H1875 — “seek”) is the Chronicler’s theological diagnostic throughout 2 Chronicles: it marked Asa (2 Chronicles 14:4), Jehoshaphat (2 Chronicles 17:3-4), Uzziah (2 Chronicles 26:5), and negatively Ahaz (not seeking). Josiah’s seeking of God is the personal foundation from which the public reform grows.
4: The demolition of Baal altars (mizbĕḥôt habĕʿālîm) and incense altars (ḥammānîm) is described with concrete actions: they were demolished in his presence, cut down, ground to powder, and scattered. The ḥammānîm (incense altars) are mentioned in Leviticus 26:30 and Isaiah 17:8 and 27:9 as idolatrous objects to be destroyed; the precise identification — possibly solar cult incense burners — is debated among archaeologists. Several small incense altars from the Iron Age II have been excavated in Judah and represent the material culture being destroyed here.
5: Burning the bones of priests on their own altars is the most extreme form of cultic defilement in the ANE: it rendered the altar permanently impure, preventing any future sacrifice. This practice is prophesied in 1 Kings 13:2 against Jeroboam’s altar at Bethel (“on you they shall burn human bones”), and Josiah’s action at Bethel is the fulfillment (2 Kings 23:16). The Chronicler’s mention of this indicates the radical irreversibility of Josiah’s purge.
6: Josiah’s reform extends into the northern territories — Manasseh, Ephraim, Simeon, and Naphtali. This is significant: after the fall of Samaria in 722 BCE, much of the north was depopulated and the territory was theoretically available to a strong Judean king. The Assyrian empire was weakening in the late 7th century, and Josiah may have effectively controlled these territories. The Chronicler presents the reform as pan-Israelite restoration — the northern tribes are implicitly re-gathered under Davidic leadership.
7: The summary of the purge uses the vocabulary of total annihilation applied to idols: altars cut down (nātaṣ), Asherah poles chopped up (kārat), carved images ground to powder (dāqaq), incense altars cut down (gādaʿ) throughout all Israel. The thoroughness mirrors the Holy War vocabulary applied to Canaanite cult objects in Deuteronomy 7:5 and 2 Chronicles 12:3. For Josiah, restoring covenant faithfulness requires the same total destruction of false worship that the conquest had originally mandated.
8: The eighteenth year of Josiah’s reign (c. 622 BCE) marks the beginning of the temple repair project that will unexpectedly yield the law-scroll discovery. The Chronicler introduces this project with a delegation: Shaphan the secretary, Maaseiah the city governor, and Joah the recorder. Shaphan becomes a pivotal figure — his family (his son Ahikam, his grandson Gedaliah) will be crucial to Jeremiah’s preservation and to the post-fall administration of Judah.
9: The money collected from “Manasseh, Ephraim, and all the remnant of Israel and from all Judah and Benjamin and the inhabitants of Jerusalem” suggests a pan-Israelite offering for the temple — even northern territories contributing to the Jerusalem sanctuary. This is the Chronicler’s consistent vision of the unified people of God gathered around the Davidic-Levitical temple. The Levites as temple-tax collectors appear in 2 Chronicles 24:5-6 (the Joash temple repair) — the Josiah project follows the same institutional pattern.
10: The workmen assigned to oversee the house of the LORD are given the money to pay skilled laborers. The pattern of temple repair funding through direct labor contracts recurs in Chronicles (cf. 2 Chronicles 24:12-13 under Joash). The system bypasses a central treasury and puts the money directly into construction, which the Chronicler notes in v11 as consistent with its purpose.
11: Craftsmen and builders use the funds for “quarried stone and timber for joinery.” The specification “beams for the buildings that the kings of Judah had neglected” (ʾăšer hizmîlû malkê yĕhûdâ) — neglected or let go to ruin — indicates the physical deterioration of the temple under Manasseh and Amon was severe. The temple’s structural condition becomes a metaphor for the spiritual condition of the kingdom: neglect under apostasy requires active repair under faithfulness.
12: The supervisors Jahath and Obadiah (from Merari) and Zechariah and Meshullam (from Kohath) represent the Levitical organization of the work. The Chronicler’s interest in proper Levitical oversight of the temple economy is consistent: temple work is sacred and must be administered by the designated clan structure established under David (1 Chr 23-26). “All who were skilled in musical instruments” adding to the supervisors suggests the Levitical singers were involved in the project administration, not just worship.
13: The administrative detail — Levites directing burden-bearers, supervising all work in every kind of service, with scribes and officials and gatekeepers — reflects the complex bureaucratic organization of a major construction project. The Levitical temple administration that David organized (1 Chr 23-27) is here functioning as the Chronicler envisioned it: the Levites as the administrative backbone of the national religious life, not merely cultic performers.
14: “Hilkiah the priest found the book of the law of the LORD given through Moses” (sēper tôrat YHWH bĕyad Mōšeh) — the discovery of the sēper hattôrâ is one of the most debated events in Old Testament historiography. The prevailing scholarly view identifies this scroll with Deuteronomy (or a proto-Deuteronomy), noting that: (1) the curses Josiah fears (v24) match Deuteronomy 28; (2) the covenant renewal that follows (vv29-33) matches Deuteronomy’s covenant format; (3) the reform measures including centralization match Deuteronomic law. How the scroll came to be in the temple — deposited there for safekeeping, hidden during Manasseh’s reign, or genuinely lost — is unknown.
15: Hilkiah’s report to Shaphan the secretary follows normal bureaucratic procedure: the high priest communicates to the royal secretary, who will transmit to the king. The scroll is described as “a book” (sēper) and then as “the book of the law” — a distinction that may indicate Hilkiah recognized it as specifically the Mosaic Torah scroll, not merely an administrative document. The physical finding during construction work (rather than in an archive) may indicate it had been stored in a wall or floor space.
16: Shaphan reports both the completion of the practical administrative tasks (“everything committed to your servants they are doing”) and the scroll discovery, treating them as two items in his briefing to the king. The scroll is “a book” in Shaphan’s initial description; it becomes “the book” after Shaphan reads it aloud. The oral reading before the king represents the standard procedure for presenting official documents — royal archives were read to kings who heard rather than personally reading documents.
17: The temple-money accounting — collected, delivered to overseers, paid to workers — is summarized as properly completed before the scroll discovery is reported. The Chronicler presents the administrative integrity of the project as the backdrop for the spiritual crisis about to unfold: a king whose practical faithfulness in temple repair led to the discovery of the very law that revealed how far Israel had fallen.
18: The public reading of the scroll before the king — wayyiqrāʾhû Šāpān lipnê hammelek — is the pivotal moment. The reading of the Torah before the king echoes Deuteronomy 17:18-20’s command that the king copy the law, read it, and follow it all his life. The king’s response to hearing the law read distinguishes the faithful king from the apostate: Josiah tears his clothes; Jehoiakim will cut Jeremiah’s scroll with a penknife and burn it (Jeremiah 36:23-24).
19: Josiah tears his clothes (wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bĕgādāyw) — the standard expression of mourning and alarm in the ANE (Genesis 37:29; 2 Samuel 1:11; 2 Kings 22:11). The tearing of clothes upon hearing the law signals that Josiah heard the curses of Deuteronomy 28 and recognized their application to Israel’s actual condition. This is precisely the response the Deuteronomic narrator approves: the covenant mediator who genuinely hears the covenantal obligations and their threatened consequences.
20: The delegation sent to inquire of YHWH includes Hilkiah the priest, Ahikam son of Shaphan (who will later protect Jeremiah, Jeremiah 26:24), Abdon son of Micah, Shaphan the secretary, and Asaiah the king’s servant. The choice to inquire of a prophet rather than simply act on the written text reflects the continuing prophetic institution as the living voice that interprets Torah for the current situation. Josiah does not presume he can read the text and apply it without prophetic guidance.
21: “Go, inquire of the LORD for me and for all who remain in Israel and Judah concerning the words of the book that has been found” — the scope is pan-Israelite: “all who remain in Israel and Judah” includes the northern population living under Josiah’s expanded control. Josiah’s identification of “great is the wrath of the LORD that is poured out on us, because our fathers have not kept the word of the LORD” shows he has already understood the text as describing Judah’s actual situation — the Deuteronomic curses are in effect.
22: Huldah the prophetess, wife of Shallum son of Tokhath — she is stationed “in Jerusalem in the Second Quarter” (bammišneh), the new urban district north or northwest of the original city, developed in the 8th–7th centuries (cf. Zephaniah 1:10 which mentions the Maktesh area and the “Second Quarter”). The delegation goes to Huldah even though Jeremiah and Zephaniah were active prophets in Jerusalem at this time. Possible explanations: her proximity, her authority in cultic-legal matters, or her specific association with the temple community (her husband was a temple official). Huldah is one of four named female prophets in the OT (Miriam, Deborah, Huldah, Noadiah).
23: “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel” (koh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl) — Huldah’s oracle uses the standard prophetic messenger formula, establishing her as an authorized spokesperson for YHWH. The formula itself claims institutional prophetic authority equal to any male prophet. Her oracle has two parts: judgment on Jerusalem (vv24-25) and mercy for Josiah personally (vv26-28). This structural distinction — the judgment for the nation is unalterable; the mercy for the individual who humbles himself is secure — reflects the Chronicler’s core theology.
24: “I will bring disaster upon this place and upon its inhabitants — all the curses written in the book that was read before the king of Judah” (kol-haqqĕlālôt hakkĕtûḇôt ʾal-hassēper ʾăšer qārĕʾû…) — the explicit identification of the scroll’s curses as the content of the coming disaster. This is the Deuteronomic curse-fulfillment mechanism of Deuteronomy 28:15-68: Israel’s persistent disobedience triggers the written consequences. The judgment is not arbitrary; it was covenantally announced centuries before and the nation had walked into it progressively through Manasseh’s generation.
25: The three-part description of the sin — “they have abandoned me” (ʿazāḇûnî), “made offerings to other gods” (wayyĕqaṭṭĕrû lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm), and “provoking me to anger with all the works of their hands” (lĕhaḵʿîsênî bĕkol maʿăśēh yĕdêhem) — matches the indictment formulae of the Deuteronomic History (1 Kings 14:9; 2 Kings 22:17). “Works of their hands” is the standard Deuteronomic phrase for idols, emphasizing their manufactured rather than divine origin. The combination “provoke to anger” (kiʿas) is a characteristically Deuteronomic description of idolatry’s effect on YHWH.
26: The second part of Huldah’s oracle shifts to Josiah personally: “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel” repeated, this time addressed to “the king of Judah who sent you to inquire of the LORD.” The structural distinction in v26-28 is between corporate judgment (national, inevitable) and personal mercy (conditional on the king’s response). This distinction runs through the Chronicler’s entire theology: community consequences flow from corporate apostasy, but individuals who genuinely seek YHWH find a different personal outcome.
27: “Because your heart was tender and you humbled yourself before God when you heard his words” (yaʿan rāk lĕḇāḇĕkā wattikkānaʿ millipnê ʾĕlōhîm…) — the three elements of Josiah’s favorable assessment: tender heart (rāk lēḇ), self-humbling (kānaʿ — H3665, the Chronicler’s key retribution-reversal word), and the specific trigger (hearing the words against this place and its inhabitants). All three elements are connected: hearing led to humbling, and humbling is the covenantal disposition that activates mercy. The word kānaʿ here joins Hezekiah (2 Chronicles 32:26), Manasseh (2 Chronicles 33:12, 19, 23), and negatively Amon, forming the Chronicler’s central diagnostic of royal faithfulness in these chapters.
28: “I will gather you to your fathers, and you will be gathered to your grave in peace” (nĕʾāsaptā ʾel-qiḇrōtêkā bĕšālôm) — the promise of a peaceful death is specifically a personal mercy, not a promise that Jerusalem will avoid judgment. Josiah does not personally witness the Babylonian destruction (he dies at Megiddo in 609 BCE). The phrase “your eyes shall not see all the disaster that I will bring upon this place” is the merciful qualification: he is spared the sight of what his faithfulness could not permanently prevent. The corporate judgment cannot be reversed; the individual’s eyes can be spared it.
29: The covenant renewal assembly begins with Josiah summoning “all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem” (kol-ziqnê yĕhûdâ wîrûšālaim). The Deuteronomic covenant-renewal format (Deuteronomy 31:9-13; Josh 24) required the assembly of the entire covenant community before the reading and re-affirmation of the Torah. This institutional pattern — king, elders, people, priests, Levites — structures the ceremony in the manner of the Mosaic and Joshuanic precedents.
30: The full assembly at the temple — men, women, and children; priests and Levites and all the people, small and great (miqqāṭōn wĕʿad-gādôl) — mirrors the language of Deuteronomy 29:10-11 (“you are standing today, all of you, before the LORD your God: the heads of your tribes, your elders, and your officers, all the men of Israel, your little ones, your wives”). The reading of the covenant “in their hearing” (bĕʾoznêhem) echoes the Mosaic reading of the book of the covenant (Exodus 24:7; Deuteronomy 31:11). The covenant renewal reconstitutes Israel as the covenant people at a critical moment before judgment.
31: “The king stood in his place and made a covenant before the LORD” (wayyaʿămōd hammelek ʿal-ʿomĕdô wayyikrōt habĕrît…) — kārat bĕrît (cut a covenant) is the foundational covenant-making formula throughout the OT. The Josiah covenant renewal is described in three components: “to follow the LORD and to keep his commandments, testimonies, and statutes with all his heart and all his soul.” The phrase “with all his heart and all his soul” (bĕkol-lĕḇāḇô ûḇĕkol-napšô) is Deuteronomy’s Shema-love formula (Deuteronomy 6:5; 2 Chronicles 10:12) — the covenantal response to YHWH’s election.
32: “He required all who were present in Jerusalem and Benjamin to stand to it” (wayyaʿămēd kol-hannimṣāʾ bîrûšālaim ûḇinyāmîn) — the covenant obligation is made corporate and binding. The “inhabitants of Jerusalem acted according to the covenant of God, the God of their fathers” completes the covenant formula: the people are the other party, and their standing to the covenant is the covenantal response to the king’s initiative. This mirrors the Mosaic structure of Sinai: Moses reads, the people hear, the people respond.
33: “Josiah removed all the abominations from all the territories belonging to the people of Israel and required all who were present in Israel to serve the LORD their God” — the territorial scope (all Israel) and the active enforcement of worship (requiring service) confirms the Chronicler’s vision of the Josiah reform as a pan-Israelite restoration. “All his days they did not turn aside from following the LORD, the God of their fathers” — the Chronicler’s closing evaluation is unqualified: under Josiah, the entire covenant community maintained YHWH-only worship. This is the last unambiguous moment of national covenant faithfulness before the Babylonian period.
